title: Baby Massage: Why and How to Do It
excerpt: Bala abhyanga, or baby massage, is one of Ayurveda's oldest gifts to new parents: a daily oil ritual that builds strong limbs, calms the nervous system, and deepens the bond between child and caregiver.
body_html:
a tradition most grandmothers already know

If you grew up in an Indian household, you probably remember being laid on someone's lap while warm oil was worked into your limbs before bath time. It felt routine then. Ayurveda has always considered it a medical necessity, not a luxury. The practice is called bala abhyanga: bala meaning child or strength, abhyanga meaning oleation or massage with oil. Classical texts devote entire chapters to it, placing it among the essential daily care routines for an infant.

The Kashyapa Samhita, the principal Ayurvedic text on paediatrics, prescribes daily oil massage from the very first days of life. It describes how the infant's body is still forming, the joints are soft, the channels are open, and the skin absorbs medicinal oils deeply. This is precisely the window when massage does the most good. The Ashtanga Hridaya, one of the three foundational grantha texts of classical medicine, similarly lists abhyanga as essential for building bala, firmness, and healthy growth in children (Ashtanga Hridaya, Uttarasthana).

the child who receives daily massage grows with stronger limbs and a more settled temperament

what the classical texts say about the benefits

Ayurvedic physicians were remarkably specific about the effects of regular massage on an infant. The Sushruta Samhita notes that oleation nourishes the skin, strengthens the underlying muscle and connective tissue, and improves the body's capacity to withstand fatigue and exertion (Sushruta Samhita, Chikitsasthana). For a baby whose entire job is growing, those are foundational gains.

The Ashtanga Hridaya explains that abhyanga pacifies vata dosha, the wind-and-movement energy that governs the nervous system, circulation, and the sense of touch (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). In infancy, vata is naturally heightened because the child has just entered the world: new sounds, new gravity, new sensations from every direction. Oil massage is the simplest way to ground vata, reduce irritability, improve sleep, and settle the restless quality that many parents recognise as colic or fussiness.

Classical texts also link regular abhyanga to better skin texture, improved joint mobility, and resistance to environmental changes. The Kashyapa Samhita notes that the child who receives daily massage grows with stronger limbs and a more settled temperament. Modern paediatric research has arrived at similar findings through different language, noting that massage improves weight gain in premature infants and reduces cortisol levels. The mechanism Ayurveda describes, which is the nourishing and stabilising action of warm medicated oil on open skin channels, aligns reasonably well with what researchers now call the stimulation of the vagal nerve pathway.

choosing an oil and finding your rhythm

Sesame oil is the most widely recommended base in classical texts because it is warming, penetrating, and naturally balancing for vata. The Ashtanga Hridaya and Kashyapa Samhita both favour sesame for general infant use, especially in cooler seasons or for babies who seem light, dry, or unsettled. For babies born in hot climates or during summer, coconut oil or a mix of sesame and coconut is gentler. Medicated oils like bala taila (prepared with the herb Sida cordifolia) are mentioned in classical paediatric care as superior when available. If you cannot access medicated oils, plain cold-pressed sesame oil is a sound classical choice.

The oil should be gently warmed: not hot, just comfortable on your inner wrist. Massage before the morning bath is the timing most consistently described across the texts. The Kashyapa Samhita recommends gentle, circular strokes on the joints and long strokes along the limbs, always moving toward the heart on the way back. Keep the room warm and free of drafts. The entire session, including the bath that follows, should feel unhurried and calm. If the baby cries persistently, stop and try again the next day. Forcing the routine defeats its purpose.

Frequency matters. Daily massage through at least the first year, and ideally the first three years, is what the classical tradition recommends. Even fifteen minutes done consistently will, over months, build the strength and settled temperament these texts describe. If daily feels too much in a busy household, aim for at least four or five times a week.

when to pause and when to see a doctor

This article is general information drawn from classical Ayurvedic texts. It is not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical advice from your paediatrician or a qualified vaidya.

Pause massage and consult your doctor if your baby has a skin infection, open wound, unexplained rash, fever, or any condition where touch causes visible distress. Do not massage a jaundiced newborn without medical guidance, and always check with your child's doctor before using any medicated or herbal oil, especially on premature infants. If your baby seems unwell, limp, or unusually difficult to soothe, that is a sign to seek professional care rather than rely on home remedies.

Try This Today

  • Warm the oil first: Pour a small amount of cold-pressed sesame oil into a steel bowl and rest the bowl in hot water for two to three minutes. Test it on your inner wrist before touching your baby.
  • Start with the legs: The classical texts suggest beginning with the limbs. Use long strokes down and circular movements around the knees and ankles. This lets the baby settle into the touch before you move to the abdomen and back.
  • Keep the room warm: Close windows and switch off fans before you begin. A cold draft on oiled skin is the most common reason babies resist the massage. Warmth in the room helps the oil absorb and keeps vata grounded.
  • Follow with a warm bath: Let the oil sit for at least ten minutes, then bathe the baby in warm water. This is the sequence prescribed in classical texts: oil first, then water, so the skin retains the nourishment rather than losing it.
  • Make it a calm ritual: Soft humming, eye contact, and a steady pace matter as much as the oil. The Kashyapa Samhita describes the caregiver's touch itself as therapeutic. Rushing through it reduces the benefit.
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: Bala abhyanga, or baby massage, is one of Ayurveda's oldest gifts to new parents: a daily oil ritual that builds strong limbs, calms the nervous system, and deepens the bond between child and caregiver.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Daily oil massage from the first days of life is a core paediatric prescription in Ayurveda, not a folk custom.
Warm sesame oil calms vata, the energy governing the nervous system, making it the classical remedy for infant fussiness and restless sleep.
Consistent bala abhyanga, done gently before the morning bath, is linked in classical texts to stronger limbs, better skin, and a more settled temperament.

glossary_terms.definition: Infant oil massage: bala means child or strength, abhyanga means oleation with oil applied through touch.
The Ayurvedic functional principle governing movement, the nervous system, and the sense of touch; naturally elevated in infancy.
A classical medicated oil prepared with Sida cordifolia, prescribed in paediatric texts for strengthening massage.
Oleation: the therapeutic use of oils or fats to nourish tissues, lubricate channels, and pacify vata.

sources: Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana (daily regimen) and Uttarasthana (paediatrics)
Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana (general therapeutics and oleation)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Bala abhyanga" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Bala abhyanga is defined as: Infant oil massage: bala means child or strength, abhyanga means oleation with oil applied through touch.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to warm the oil first. Pour a small amount of cold-pressed sesame oil into a steel bowl and rest the bowl in hot water for two to three minutes. Test it on your inner wrist before touching your baby.

faqs.question: Are there any specific lifestyle habits I should adopt?
faqs.answer: A key suggestion is to start with the legs. The classical texts suggest beginning with the limbs. Use long strokes down and circular movements around the knees and ankles. This lets the baby settle into the touch before you move to the abdomen and back.
